data.title: Eastern Bankshares: Growing Through Acquisitions, Attractive Value
data.description:
We continue our critical Q2 earnings season review of regional bank performance today with Eastern Bankshares, Inc. (NASDAQ:EBC). Thus far, regional banks have had better-than-expected earnings. We like examining regional banks to get a better understanding of what is happening with local economies on the ground. On average, loans and deposits are growing, margins have stabilized and are higher than historical averages, the cost of funds is coming down, and we have a more friendly administration for banks here in 2025. Thus far, it has been a great earnings season.

We follow Eastern Bankshares, and this one is a bit unique in that it has grown through mergers and thereby increased its loan and deposit base inorganically. Most recently, it is bringing HarborOne Bancorp (HONE) into the fold.

All things considered, the key metrics here are strong, and we do like the acquisitions being made for the long term, so we think shares are a long-term buy here. Let us discuss the just-reported Q2 earnings.

Eastern Bankshares' Q2 sees strong headline results

The top line was up big from last year, coming as no surprise due to its merger activity that we mentioned above. Here in Q2, revenues were $244.9 million, up 59% from last year. This was, however, a beat on estimates of $3.6 million. Let us dig into the key metrics, which we view as mixed here relative to the sequential Q1. Deposits ticked up 2% here to $21.22 billion, versus $20.80 billion at the end of Q1. Loans were also up 2% sequentially to $18.59 billion versus $18.2 billion in Q1. Deposits grew primarily due to increases in municipal government deposits.

How about margins? For Eastern Bankshares, the bank saw a very strong 21 basis point expansion on net interest margin to 3.59% from 3.38% in the sequential quarter. Further, the margins are up 95 basis points from a year ago. This is very strong. This led to an expansion in net interest income to $202.0 million from $188.9 million. Non-interest income also expanded to $42.9 million. Factoring in operating expenses, we saw a nice increase in net income. Net income was $81.7 million, or $0.41 per share, compared to net income of $67.5 million, or $0.34 per share, in Q1. This was a solid beat of $0.03 versus estimates. How about asset quality?

Eastern Bankshares' asset quality

There was only a minor provision for credit losses. In Q2, Eastern Bankshares had a provision for credit losses of $7.6 million, but this was up from $6.6 million in Q3. Eastern Bank shares also slightly increased the allowance for credit losses to $232.1 million from $224.3 million. That was an increase from 1.25% of total loans to 1.27% of total loans. That said, non-performing loans were just 0.30% of total loans versus 0.51% in Q1. So this is an improvement. Further, net charge-offs were actually a credit this quarter of $0.2 million in recoveries compared to $11.2 million of charge-offs in Q1, or 0.26% of average total loans. That is also positive.

On top of this, the return metrics are all improving from Q1. The adjusted return on average assets was 1.30%, a significant improvement from 1.09% in Q1. Further, the operating return on tangible equity surged to 13.56% from 11.70% in the sequential quarter. This was driven by a considerable improvement in operating efficiency. The operating efficiency ratio improved to 50.8%, a 290 basis point improvement from 53.7% last quarter, and the best this metric has been in many quarters.

Valuation and shareholder-friendly

One reason that we see a soft Buy rating as appropriate is that while there is a lot of uncertainty now with another, and rather larger, merger with HarborOne, the valuation is getting attractive. Without considering that future merger, the stock is trading at a discount to its book value and only a slight premium to its tangible book value. At $15.9 per share, EBC stock is cheap versus the $17.42 book value and attractive relative to the 12.53 tangible book value. Of course, most banks we have covered are seeing significant premiums right now, so we think there is an opportunity here.

Further, the bank pays a dividend of $0.13 per share, or over 3% yield. On top of that, it is buying back shares. Eastern repurchased 183,053 shares in the quarter at an average price of $16.36 for an aggregate purchase price of $3.0 million.

Take-home

There is uncertainty with yet another merger coming, but this inorganic growth has been paying off. Margins are stronger. Earnings power is up. There is a nice dividend. Asset quality is mostly positive. Repurchases always help, and the stock relative to book value is attractive. We think EBC makes for one to consider for a long-term investment.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Power Solutions, Porsche, B2Gold And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between July 18–July 24.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?


Power Solutions: Projected Growth Points To Further Upside

Steady Quant | Buy

Power Solutions (NASDAQ:PSIX) is an exciting rising star this year. Its stock price has skyrocketed. In this article, I will examine its growth trend and valuation status after the year-to-date price surge. I will also check its liquidity profile, which is important for small-cap stocks. From what I see, the current P/E of 24.7x still doesn't fully reflect Power Solutions' growth potential, and its liquidity risk is totally manageable. So I think the stock is still worth buying at this level.


Canadian Natural Will See Its Already Impressive Margins Boosted By LNG Canada

Louis Gerard | Strong Buy

Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) (TSX:CNQ:CA) stands as a bastion of Canadian Energy, being one of the largest independent O&G producers in the country. It currently has a vast set of assets, mainly in Western Canada, and has become a key player in the energy landscape.

With the development of LNG Canada and the sound economics of the legacy oil sands business (even if I admit that I'm not the biggest expert on Canadian oil sands), I believe that Canadian Natural Resources is poised to gain an advantage of the Canadian energy landscape


CRISPR Therapeutics: Shares Are Soaring - This Could Be The Start Of Something Big

Edmund Ingham | Buy

Back in early May, I shared a note with Seeking Alpha readers on CRISPR Therapeutics AG (NASDAQ:CRSP), advising shareholders and prospective investors in the company to "ignore the gloom, stay the course".

That has proven to be good advice, as two and a half months later, stock is up nearly 90% since that note, with shares trading at a value of $66 premarket today—their highest value since early February 2024, a couple of weeks after CRISPR Therapeutics and partner Vertex (VRTX) secured a historic first approval for a CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing therapy, Casgevy, in the indication of Sickle Cell Disease (SCD). ...

In this latest update, I'll try to unpack what—other than insider buys—has been driving CRISPR Therapeutics' valuations skywards—and how much higher I believe the stock could climb.


CCIF: The ~20% Illusion And What Carlyle's Isn't Telling You

Pioneering Ideas | Sell

Carlyle Credit Income Fund (NYSE:CCIF) is a publicly traded closed-end fund that primarily invests in equity and junior debt tranches of U.S. collateralized loan obligations (CLOs). Managed by Carlyle Global Credit Investment Management, CCIF seeks to generate high current income and capital appreciation by leveraging Carlyle's scale and expertise in structured credit.

Previously operating as the Vertical Capital Income Fund (VCIF), the fund was acquired by Carlyle in July 2023. Following the acquisition, the fund was rebranded as CCIF, and its investment strategy pivoted from residential mortgages toward U.S. CLO equity and junior debt. Leveraging its scale as one of the largest CLO managers globally, Carlyle repositioned the portfolio to capitalize on its structured credit platform, which manages over $50 billion in CLO assets.

The fund currently pays a monthly dividend of 10.5 cents a share, translating to a mind-boggling dividend yield of 19% on its July 18th closing price of $6.49. The yield is attractive, but it comes at a price. We therefore had a look at potential risk factors that may impact CCIF's capacity to continue delivering these yields to its investors.


Solid Power Stock Surges Higher: What Should Investors Expect Going Forward?

Chris Lau | Hold

Last Friday, July 18, 2025, Solid Power (NASDAQ:SLDP) jumped by 17%. The stock returned 25% since I last wrote about the company over two years ago. By comparison, the S&P 500 (IVV) performed better, returning 58.41%.

The pre-revenue, research and development firm in the battery sector benefited from a surge in QuantumScape (QS) shares. QS stock gained for seven straight days and gained over 150% in the last month.

QuantumScape posted a major milestone. As Seeking Alpha News reported, it successfully integrated its advanced Cobra separator process into baseline cell production.

At the time of writing this article, QS stock is down by over 15%. What impact could QS's stock action have on Solid Power’s stock performance? Did Solid Power’s update in May justify the stock rising from around $1.15 to nearly $4.00 a share?

YMAX: This Fund-Of-Funds Could See Sustained Upside

Chetan Woodun | Buy

The worst seems over for the YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAX), which after seeing its NAV erode by around 40% since being incepted on January 16 last year is on an uptrend and trades at around $13.95 as charted below.

This thesis aims to show that this uptrend is likely to be sustained for an option strategy that pays a yield of nearly 59% at a time when interest rates look likely to be cut in September. On top, President Trump wants to replace the Federal Chairman with someone more dovish, but this may cause concern about the U.S. Central Bank losing its independence, resulting in volatility, an important ingredient for YMAX.

However, this fund, which is designed to simplify ownership of YieldMax ETFs by providing investors with exposure to a broad range of investment opportunities under a single fund, comes with risks, making it important to determine its mechanics.


B2Gold: Production Ramps To Drive Above-Average Growth

David Zanoni | Strong Buy

B2Gold (NYSE:BTG) (TSX:BTO:CA) has many things going for it. This gold miner is still valued attractively with strong expected revenue and earnings growth. The stock is in a new positive uptrend on the long-term monthly chart. B2Gold is ramping up production in the Goose mine in Q3 2025, which can be a strong positive catalyst for the company. Additional production is also expected from other key mines this year.


Power Solutions International: Solid Margins, Fuel Flexibility, And A Market Still Ignoring The Cash Flow

CAN Analyst | Strong Buy

Power Solutions International (PSIX) is a small capitalization industry technology company that has long been outside trader radar. However, in 2025 the situation has fundamentally changed: the company has returned to strong profitability, reduced debt, established itself in fast-growing segments (especially in data centers and energy), and began to create long-term value through innovation from HVO-ready engines to lithium-ion electrification solutions.

Most investors still look at PSIX as a risky player, mainly due to fluctuating share price, which happens due to low float, but the facts show the opposite: the company has become one of the most effective in its segment. With fast-growing margins, stable cash flows. PSIX becomes a valuable "hidden" opportunity for long-term growth.


Opendoor Technologies: Time To Sell The Rally

Ian Bezek | Sell

Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) stock has certainly been on an impressive run recently, with the stock quadrupling over the past month. ...

This is quite the move coming from a company that many people had left for dead not long ago.

There were concerns that the company might be delisted due to its share price falling well below a dollar. Opendoor had already sought authorization for a reverse stock split. And the company's earnings results had been in a clear downtrend as well.

With this huge rebound in OPEN stock, however, investors are reconsidering their views of this real estate company. So, is there any real merit to this recent rebound or should traders be looking to fade the move? I'm in the bearish camp. There's not much here to explain the recent run. And for investors that currently own OPEN stock, this is a great time to consider lightening up on that position.


SMH: Buying This ETF Right Now Will Make You Shake Your Head - Sell

Doodad Capital | Strong Sell

The VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH) is a fund that seeks to replicate the performance of the underlying index—MVIS US Listed Semiconductor 25 Index. These companies are all focused on the production, design, and manufacturing of semiconductors, which has been a popular investment sector over the past few years due to the AI wave. ...

Despite being a huge investment trend over the past few years to invest in chip companies, we believe that at the current moment, there is room for a dip, as we will outline in the section below.


Devon Energy's Stock Swoon: Could This Permian Producer Become A Prime M&A Target?

Michael Fitzsimmons | Buy

The stock of Oklahoma-based oil and gas producer Devon Energy (NYSE:DVN) is down 32% over the past year - much greater than its bigger peers (see one-year total returns chart below). The downdraft in the stock is despite the fact that Devon grew Q1 production to 815,000 boe/d (+22.7% yoy) on the heels of its Grayson Mill Energy acquisition in September of last year. Yet in my opinion, and while DVN is delivering decent free cash flow and shareholder returns, the company simply doesn't have the "size-n-scale" to be an outstanding long-term O&G investment. Devon's market cap is now $21 billion, and with net-debt of $7.8 billion has a current total enterprise value of only ~$28.8 billion. That being the case, the best and most likely outcome for DVN shareholders would be if Devon agreed to merge with a bigger and more diversified company. And I suspect that's exactly what's likely to happen as the bigger O&G companies continue to consolidate the sector. On the basis of this observation, and Devon's strong free cash profile (see below), I rate the company a Buy with a $42 price target. Any possible buyout offer could potentially take place in a range of $50-52/share.


Porsche: Getting Lamborghini And Robotaxi Exposure Basically For Free

WideAlpha | Strong Buy

Charlie Munger famously said, "No matter how wonderful a business is, it’s not worth an infinite price". He also used to advise to "invert, always invert" to see situations from a different perspective. Combined, these suggest a powerful truth: some bad investments can become good at a low enough price, and good investments can become extraordinary if purchased at a bargain price.

Finding such opportunities is not easy in today's highly priced markets. Still, if one looks around the world, there are some situations that offer deep value. One example is Porsche Automobil Holding SE (OTCPK:POAHY)(OTCPK:POAHF), which is not just a value story, but value squared, or a discount on top of a discount. Porsche SE's main asset is its 31.9% ownership of Volkswagen's (OTCPK:VWAGY)(OTCPK:VLKAF) capital, followed by its 12.5% ownership of Porsche AG's capital.

Porsche SE trades at a 27% discount to its net asset value (NAV). Its largest holding, Volkswagen, is itself trading at a deep discount to tangible book value. Volkswagen’s market capitalization is effectively covered by its public stakes in companies like Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG (OTCPK:DRPRY)(OTCPK:DRPRF), Traton SE (OTCPK:TRATY)(OTCPK:TRATF), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), QuantumScape Corporation (QS), Sinotruk and Gotion. This leaves very valuable assets, including its full ownership of Lamborghini, Audi, and approximately €43 billion of equity in its financial services division valued at basically zero, not to mention the core brands, including the namesake VW, Škoda, and SEAT/CUPRA that have challenges but remain profitable.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: The Saga Continues
data.description:
Overview

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (NYSE:TSM) is a large capitalization equity operating within the Information Technology sector that stands out as the clear leader in the global semiconductor fabrication industry. Recently (July 17th, 2025), TSM reported stellar Q2'25 earnings results, beating on both top-line and bottom-line estimates and upwardly revising FY2025 guidance, reaffirming the company's position as an industry titan. In my opinion, though, a significant opportunity lies ahead. Amid a robust earnings print, fiscal stimulus, and a global up-cycle in semiconductor demand, the stock experienced a brief technical pullback and simultaneously hosted a highly liquid options market that permitted exploitation of well-documented post-earnings announcement phenomena.

In this article, I break down Q2 financial performance, elaborate upon macroeconomic tailwinds, analyze recent price action, and explore how this confluence sets an asymmetric proverbial stage for a tactical, bullish options play.

Q2'25: The Moat Widens

TSMC reported strong financial performance in the second quarter of FY2025, showcasing robust top- and bottom-line prints, lean operations, and clear outperformance relative to prior guidance. On a top-line basis, the company delivered consolidated revenue of NT$933.79 billion (US$30.07B), which represents both a 38.6% YoY increase and a 11.3% QoQ increase. Notably, these results substantially surpassed management's revenue forecast range of US$28.4B to US$29.2B (2.98%-5.88% outperformance). Remarkably, the company achieved such growth without compromising efficiency, as margins remained in line with previous Q2 guidance (gross margin of 58.6%, forecast of 57%-59%, and operating margin of 49.6% slightly exceeding the range of 47% to 49%). Deterministically, the variation in performance results can be explained by growth in semiconductor demand. In 2024, global semiconductor sales topped $627B (19% above 2023 sales), and Deloitte expects 2025 sales to exceed $697B, thus signifying an 11.2% growth rate.

As demand for semiconductors expands (largely for AI and High-Performance Computing related use), customers increasingly require advanced technological capabilities (defined as 7nm and below). Today, TSMC stands as the singular foundry that can meet such technological demand. Evidently, this is demonstrated in the following graphic, which shows that 74% of advanced wafer fabrication accounts for 74% of the company's revenue.

As mentioned, TSMC's advanced microchip output is largely geared toward HPC-related applications. Between AI-driven services, data centers, and accelerated computing, this industry now accounts for the majority of end-user demand (60% according to TSMC Investor Relations, as was disclosed in Q2'25 financial results). This complements a 14% QoQ growth in its HPC segment and highlights secular momentum in a high-growth space (bullish projections as high as 9.2% through 2032).

Ultimately, TSMC has historically controlled a substantial majority of global foundry capacity, holding roughly 62% of global market share in Q2'24 (PatentPC) and 67% in Q1'25 reports (Taipei Times). Now, while industry-wide numbers are not yet finalized, by the rules of Monopoly, I argue that market share will remain elevated through this future growth.

Policy Tailwinds

TSMC is uniquely positioned to benefit from a highly conducive macroeconomic environment, as the culmination of legislation (CHIP & Science Act and OBBBA), dollar devaluation, and imminent monetary easing provides a beneficial backdrop. Specifically, the CHIPs Act aims to incentivize onshoring of semiconductor manufacturing through subsidized loans and a 25% tax credit, per Financial Times, particularly ensuring $11.6B of funding for TSMC's U.S. fabs. Meanwhile, the recently enacted One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) elevated the aforementioned 25% tax credit up to 35% for chip production placed into service before 2033, thus further accelerating the onshoring process.

Furthermore, a gradually weakening U.S. dollar creates a favorable international play for export-driven companies that report in foreign currencies. In other words, TSMC's dollar-denominated operations through large U.S.-based clients like Nvidia and Apple translate into more favorable NT$ figures. In addition, according to several groundbreaking researchers (e.g., Oxford Open Economics and ECB), dollar weakening historically leads to improved global financial situations and improved risk sentiment. In the case of TSMC, this supports earnings momentum and can lead to significant multiple expansion.

Finally and perhaps most importantly, we have yet to see significant monetary easing via Fed rate cuts. Their imminent arrival should lower borrowing costs and, thus, stimulate capex activity and, ultimately, growth.

Textbook Technical Analysis

Catalyzed by robust Q2 financial results, TSM shares gapped up roughly 3.5% to a new ATH prior to retracing 6.7% (from peak to trough) in the days that followed. This sharp pullback effectively filled the earnings-induced gap and now offers investors a compelling entry point after peeking behind the corporate veil and into the company's contemporaneous financial performance.

In regard to volume, daily trading volume appeared to peak on earnings announcement day and gradually decreased thereafter. Plus, the recent daily candlestick suggests a potential bullish reversal, as it bears a body at the upper end (albeit large, so I cannot legitimately call it a 'hammer candle') and a lower shadow. In flux, this price-volume analysis potentially suggests that initial downward pressure from sellers may be exhaustively handing the reins back to buyers.

Academically, there are numerous studies that explore price behavior following earnings-induced gap fills. Notably, many empirically recognize the tendency of prices to revert to their pre-gap-fill trend (Daniel, Hirshleifer & Subrahmanyam), especially in the context of positive earnings surprises, and a greater amount that formally recognizes the statistical significance of PEAD (Post-Earnings Announcement Drift) (Bernard & Thomas), the tendency for stock price to continuously move in the direction of the earnings surprise for several weeks/months following announcement. Thus, the conflux of these phenomena and recent behavioral inefficiencies offers a convincing long-term opportunity.

A visible downside of this opportunity, however, is unfavorable seasonality. According to an expansive study by LPL Financial, September has historically proven to be the worst calendar month for stock market results, and specifically mentions August (sometimes noted as the 2nd or 3rd worst) as a precursor month. In my opinion, though, this theory lacks an economic foundation and is fundamentally driven by a data-mining bias. In reality, this can be countered by other data-driven studies, such as research by Alliance Bernstein or Morgan Stanley explaining historical stock performance in periods after deep drawdowns similar to what the market experienced in April's tariff-induced panic. Ultimately, the pursuit of outsized returns inherently comes in tandem with risks.

Portfolio Implementation (Drift in Price and in Volatility)

In addition to the price-based PEAD phenomenon that I previously discussed, there is a well-known occurrence that volatility generally collapses immediately following earnings announcements. However, it remains relatively underappreciated that there remains a downward PEAD in volatility (not just price) for weeks beyond the initial collapse, thus reinforcing the implication that forward-looking implied volatility frequently overstates realized volatility. There exist several studies that document this anomaly, such as that by Dubinsky & Johannes in their research on the behavior of equity options following earnings announcements.

The two drift-related phenomena, in concert with bullish fundamentals and price action, provide supporting evidence for entering a long delta, short volatility position. Advantageously, TSM maintains a fluid options market that can allow expression of such a perception. Personally, I suggest implementation via an ATM short put. The premium for selling the put frequently proves to be elevated, and the proximity of the strike to the current underlying price is directly correlated with the amount of extrinsic value (in other words, ATM has the most extrinsic value, thus the highest theta decay). This options strategy is a bullish structure in that it is both long delta and includes the possibility of assignment, which will desirably lead to stock ownership. Following is the visual distribution of extrinsic value relative to the distance of strike prices from the money:

Conclusion

To summarize, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's recent quarterly results evidently reaffirm its leadership position in the semiconductor fabrication space. These solid fundamentals, charged with strong contemporary tailwinds that are primed to strengthen amid potentially imminent rate cuts, together present a compelling long-term opportunity. For investors seeking a tactical entry point, this technical reset offers a cost-attractive setup, and a voluminous options market provides the liquidity required for expressive implementation. Personally, I am very bullish on the stock; I have been for a while and will likely continue to be for the foreseeable future. Therefore, I recommend TSM as a "Strong Buy".

data.title: Nestle: Expected Returns Are Lower Than Bonds
data.description:
Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) (OTCPK:NSRGF) shares dropped 6% after reporting disappointing half-year results. Revenue continues to decline, organic growth is driven solely from pricing, and margins remain pressured.

It's hard to sugarcoat this. Even when looking at Nestlé's 'underlying' and 'organic' figures, almost all arrows are pointing down.

This leads to the question, what do bulls see here? I know what the bears see, that's for sure.

Let's dive in.

The Nestlé Bear Thesis

I've been covering Nestlé on Seeking Alpha since early 2023. Initially, I thought that it was an attractive low-risk investment, as I expected growth to accelerate after the post-pandemic trough.

Over time, it became clear to me that Nestlé's growth and margin issues aren't a story of tough comparisons and pandemic pullforward. In fact, it was a story of saturated markets and intrinsic limitations.

Simply put, companies that don't grow revenues and earnings have very few avenues to keep up with the market. And companies that trade at relatively high valuations ahead of a three-year stagnation are very likely to not even keep up with bonds.

That is, in a nutshell, the story of Nestlé of the past three years and why I remain bearish, even after the appointment of a new CEO.

I just think that the company's issues are structural, and unless a very aggressive CEO comes in and takes almost a private-equity-like approach to a turnaround, I continue to believe the stock will provide poor returns.

First Half Results Show Things Are Getting Worse, Not Better

In the first half of 2025, Nestlé sales were CHF 44.2 billion, down 1.8% Y/Y, with organic growth of 2.9% offset by a 4.7% FX headwind.

Gross margin contracted 60 bps, while underlying trading operating margin dropped 90 bps.

This has led to a 10% decline in net profit, a 9% decline in EPS, and a 42% drop in free cash flow.

If you're a glass-half-full kind of person, you might say that FX was the primary reason for the decline and that organic growth was decent. However, when unpacking the numbers, we note that pricing is once again the main driver of organic growth, while RIG (volume + mix) is back in the negative.

Looking at the different categories, it's notable that the main drivers of organic growth are Confectionery, and Powdered & Liquid Beverages, with the rest of the categories, except water, below total group numbers.

It's not a coincidence that the two categories saw the biggest margin declines, down 330 bps and 190 bps, respectively. High coffee and cocoa prices have led to significant price increases across the industry, but not enough to offset the cost pressures.

Looking at the rest of the categories, Water might be the only bright spot, with improving margins alongside positive RIG. Other than that, all categories are showing signs of struggle.

Looking For Signs Of Change Under New CEO

After going through the results above, it's hard for me to understand how the company opened its presentation with the following slide:

I mean, revenue dropped 3%, margins declined about a 100 bps, earnings were down 11%, and net debt surpassed CHF 60 billion primarily to fund buybacks that have been value destructive for years.

And yet, it was a good half?

As I wrote in the past, the first step in fixing and turning around a company is acknowledging the problems, and I don't think Nestlé is doing it.

They do have a plan for cost savings, but I would expect, if they were really ambitious, that we would have seen significant layoffs by now. So far, we can't see these savings in the margin, that's for sure.

They also have a plan to focus on their winning SKUs, but we've seen no divestitures since the new CEO appointment. In some sense, their talking about a possible divestment of certain vitamin units is perhaps the most positive sign in the whole presentation, if you ask me.

Expected Returns

If you read my articles before, this is a bit repetitive, but I think it's important to emphasize. My investing view is quite simple. If a company doesn't grow revenue and earnings faster than market average, the chances of it providing market-beating returns, or even market-like returns, are slim.

Nestlé is targeting, in the medium term, to achieve 4%+ organic sales growth, with the caveat of "in normal market conditions". It's also targeting a minor 50 bps underlying trading operating profit margin improvement. Needless to say, even if those targets are achieved, Nestlé won't be growing in line with the market.

Further, with the historical strength of the Swiss Frank, these adjusted figures are typically much better than reported numbers.

So, if you agree with the notion that growth is the main driver of expected returns, it's hard to justify investing in Nestlé, even if you believe it will execute on its plan.

Valuation

At $93 per ADR, or CHF 74 per Swiss-listed share, Nestlé trades around 17 to 18 times earnings.

On the surface, this isn't a stretched valuation. However, when adjusting for growth, it's still a rich valuation. I mean, how do you calculate a PEG (price to earnings to growth ratio) when earnings are declining?

And, again, even if they reach the medium-term targets, this is still trading at a 3x+ PEG.

Conclusion

Nestlé has a dominant position in a saturated market, with very few avenues to deliver consistent growth, let alone market-beating growth.

Investing in the stock is often justified by its supposed lower risk, dividend track record, or geographic diversification.

However, as we've demonstrated, those things did little to help it keep up with the returns of bonds, much less equity indexes.

So, I reiterate a 'Hold' rating and continue to encourage investors to allocate their money elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Realty Income: Best Time In 10 Years To Buy
data.description:
O stock: chart of the day

I last wrote on Realty Income (NYSE:O) stock on June 7. That article was titled “Realty Income Is Better Than Simon Property After U.S. Credit Rating Downgrade”. It rated O as a buy after a comparative analysis against SPG. Since then, a few new developments call for a follow-up analysis on the stock. The rest of this article will concentrate on the top 2 developments on my radar: the stock’s latest dividend declarations and its upcoming FQ2 earnings report (ER).

Examining these new developments has led me to conclude that the current conditions surrounding the stock present the best time to buy the stock in at least 10 years, leading to a reiteration of my buy thesis. I will present various data points to support this thesis in the subsequent sections. But I think the chart that best encapsulates my argument is the one below. This chart illustrates the dividend yield spread of Realty Income relative to the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), used to approximate the overall REIT space in the past 10 years from 2016. As seen, over this period, the average spread has been 0.478%. The spread current is hovering around 1.712%, not only far above the historical average but also near the peak levels in 10 years, indicating O's unusually attractive valuation relative to the sector.

Next, I will explain why the attractiveness is even stronger given the latest dividend declarations and also the FQ2 earnings forecast.

O stock: latest dividend declarations

Realty Income is well known for its regular monthly dividends. Indeed, since my last writing, the company has declared a new dividend payout of $0.2690 per share for July and August, with an ex-dividend date of July 1 and August 1, respectively. This latest payout represents a 0.18% quarter-over-quarter increase from the $0.2685 paid from April through June 2025 and a 2.28% year-over-year increase from the same period in 2024.

Investors should not be under-impressed by the seemingly small growth rates. As illustrated above, its yield spread relative to VNQ is already near a 10-year peak on a TTM basis. With the latest payout raises—albeit small increases—the yield spread could be even thicker in terms of an FWD basis.

Further, as detailed next, the latest data also point to a lower payout ratio and thus more room for future dividend growth.

O stock: latest data suggest a lower FFO payout ratio

The company is set to announce its next earnings on August 6, 2025, with a normalized FFO estimate of $1.06 as seen in the next chart. Based on this data and also the monthly payout of $0.2685 per share in the second quarter of 2025, as shown above, the implied FFO payout ratio (on a normalized basis) for Q2 would be about 75.9%. As a background, the company reported a normalized FFO of $1.05 for Q1 of 2025. With the first three monthly payouts, the implied FFO payout ratio (again on a normalized basis) for Q1 is 75.8%.

These recent and FWD payout ratios are notably lower than their long-term averages, signaling more room for dividend growth in the years to come. To wit, the chart below displays Realty Income’s cash dividend payout ratio in the past decade, since 2016. The payout ratio has experienced some fluctuations over the years but has stayed in a relatively stable range of 72% and 88% most of the time, demonstrating the consistency of its business model. Overall, the average payout ratio is 79.00% in the past 10 years, and the FWD payout ratio I just estimated is toward the lower end of this historical spectrum.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of valuation, the stock is quite reasonably valued at a FWD P/FFO ratio of 13.6x in my view. But I think dividend yield is a more telling metric given its incredible track record of dividend growth. Along this line, the chart provided below shows Realty Income’s dividend yield in the past 10 years. The yield has stayed within a relatively stable range between around 3.00% and 6% most of the time, as seen, with an average of 4.46%. O's current TTM dividend yield stands at 5.49%, and the forward dividend yield is 5.56% based on the latest declaration. Both are again far above the historical average and close to the peak seen in the past 10 years.

On the negative side, O’s growth strategy relies heavily on acquisitions. Its debt had risen substantially in recent years, with long-term debt rising from about $15 billion in 2021 to the current level of almost $25 billion. As a reflection, its balance sheet has become more stretched since ~2021. As a reflection, the chart below shows its debt-to-equity ratio in the past 10 years. As seen, before 2021~2022, the ratio has remained below the 10-year average of 0.413 most of the time. There has been an upward trend in the debt-to-equity ratio, with values generally increasing since late 2021. Currently, O's debt-to-equity ratio stands at 0.518, not only far above its historical average but also near the highest leverage levels in the past decade. This is an area that I will closely monitor in its Q2 ER. A piece of good news on this front is that the chance of interest rate cuts has been increasing, as indicated by the data provided by the CME Group FedWatch Tool, which could help to lower O’s interest expense.

I started with a bigger picture by benchmarking O’s valuation against the overall REIT sector. To further drive the point, let me close with an even bigger picture by benchmarking the REIT sector’s valuation against other market sectors with the following dashboard (BTW, it is a Google sheet and you are welcome to download it). It’s a tool our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) uses to monitor the macroscopic picture for our members. As seen, the REIT sector, as approximated by VNQ, is currently the second most attractively valued sector in terms of its yield Z-score, only after small caps.

With this, let me close by reiterating my buy thesis. To recap, the key developments examined here are the stock’s latest dividend declarations and its latest earnings revisions for the FQ2 ER. These developments have led me to see a usual valuation discount both by horizontal and vertical comparison, and also further dividend growth potential.
